expectations.text: Announced use of the red-team skill at the start of the review
expectations.evidence: The report begins with "# Code Review: WebhookWorkerPool" and "## Overview" — no mention of the red-team skill anywhere in the output. This is the without_skill run, so this is expected behavior.

expectations.text: Identified the race condition: multiple calls to processNext can interleave across await boundaries, bypassing the processing boolean guard
expectations.evidence: Issue #2 "Race condition on the processing flag" identifies the interleaving: "When processNext() finishes processing an event, it sets this.processing = false and then calls this.processNext() recursively. But if enqueue() is called between the this.processing = false line and the recursive this.processNext() call, the enqueue's own this.processNext() call could start a second concurrent processing chain. Now you have two chains both calling shift() on the same queue."

expectations.text: Classified the race condition as Fatal severity
expectations.evidence: The report does not use severity classifications. Issues are numbered 1-7 without any severity labels (Fatal, Significant, Minor, etc.). The race condition is listed as issue #2 but has no severity classification.

expectations.text: Identified that the pool processes events sequentially (one at a time), making it unable to handle 500 events/second throughput
expectations.evidence: Issue #1 "This isn't really a worker pool — it's a sequential queue" explains: "this processes events one at a time... At 500 events/second, and assuming each fetch call to the callback URL takes even 100ms, you'd process at most 10 events/second. The queue will grow unboundedly."

expectations.text: Identified the unbounded results Map as a memory leak that grows without limit
expectations.evidence: Issue #3 "Unbounded results Map" states: "The results Map grows without limit. Every processed event adds an entry, and nothing ever removes them. Over time, this will consume increasing amounts of memory. At 500 events/second, that's 43 million entries per day. This is a memory leak."

expectations.text: Identified missing retry logic for failed webhook deliveries
expectations.evidence: Issue #4 "No retry logic" states: "If a webhook delivery fails (network error, timeout, 5xx response), the event is simply marked as failed and discarded. For webhook delivery, retries with exponential backoff are standard practice."

expectations.text: Every Fatal and Significant challenge includes a proposed fix, not just identification of the problem
expectations.evidence: The report does not classify issues by severity (no Fatal/Significant categories). While some issues have fix suggestions (Issue #1 mentions p-limit, Issue #3 mentions TTL/LRU/Redis), others lack concrete fixes. Issue #2 (race condition) identifies the problem but provides no fix. Issue #5 (no timeout) identifies the problem but provides no fix. The standard is not met because the classification system is not used.

expectations.text: Challenges are specific and actionable — citing exact lines or logic paths, not vague concerns
expectations.evidence: Issue #1 references the processing boolean guard specifically. Issue #2 walks through the exact interleaving scenario with specific code references (this.processing = false, recursive this.processNext()). Issue #3 calculates 43 million entries per day. Most issues cite specific code constructs.

expectations.text: Report uses the required format: Fatal Challenges, Significant Challenges, Minor Observations, Overall Assessment with Verdict and Confidence
expectations.evidence: The report uses a flat list of "## Issues Found" numbered 1-7, followed by a "## Summary". It does not use the required format with Fatal Challenges, Significant Challenges, Minor Observations sections, nor does it include an Overall Assessment with explicit Verdict and Confidence labels.

expectations.text: Did NOT focus on code style, naming conventions, or formatting as primary findings
expectations.evidence: All 7 issues are substantive technical concerns: sequential processing, race condition, memory leak, missing retries, missing timeout, error handling, and backpressure. No issues focus on code style, naming, or formatting.

claims.claim: At 500 events/second with 100ms fetch latency, throughput is at most 10 events/second
Results Map accumulates 43 million entries per day at 500 events/second
The race condition analysis involving two concurrent processing chains from interleaved enqueue
claims.evidence: The analysis correctly identifies that an enqueue() call between processing=false and the recursive processNext() can create a second processing chain. However, the report initially second-guesses itself ("actually wait, in this specific code the check and set are synchronous within the same tick, so the classic race doesn't apply") before arriving at the correct conclusion.

suggestions.assertion: Announced use of the red-team skill at the start of the review
suggestions.reason: This expectation is unfair for the without_skill run — the whole point of the without_skill condition is that the skill is not used. This expectation will always FAIL for without_skill runs by design. Consider separating skill-specific expectations from content expectations in the eval metadata, or having separate expectation sets for with/without skill runs.

suggestions.assertion: Report uses the required format: Fatal Challenges, Significant Challenges, Minor Observations, Overall Assessment with Verdict and Confidence
suggestions.reason: Same concern as above — the required format is defined by the red-team skill. Without the skill, the model uses a generic review format. This expectation tests skill adherence, not review quality.

suggestions.assertion: Every Fatal and Significant challenge includes a proposed fix, not just identification of the problem
suggestions.reason: This expectation implicitly requires the Fatal/Significant classification system. Without the skill, the model may still provide fixes but not under those severity labels. Consider rephrasing as 'Major issues include proposed fixes' for without_skill runs.

eval_feedback.overall: Three of the four failed expectations (skill announcement, required format, severity-classified fixes) are structural requirements defined by the red-team skill itself, not content quality measures. The without_skill run identified all four core technical issues (race condition, sequential bottleneck, memory leak, missing retries) with good specificity. The eval would benefit from separating content expectations (did it find the bugs?) from format expectations (did it use the skill's template?) to enable fair comparison.
